title: Chapter Six: The Road
content:
## JEONG YEON-SHIN

The late cold froze the dawn air on the mountain behind Jeong Estate.

If the wind through the valley had a colour, it would be the grey-white of old bone. Yeon-shin stood among the freshly turned graves and looked at what remained of his family. Seeds that would sprout next year, the steward had said. Yeon-shin thought they looked like furrows in a field no one would tend.

All night they had carried the dead. Yeon-shin, the steward, a handful of surviving workers, and the Zhongnan masters led by his second brother. Everyone covered in dirt — hands, knees, faces. The earth clung to them like a second skin. The mountain did not want to let the dead go.

Corpses.

His niece Hye-ah lay curled on a blanket to one side, alternating between sobs and fitful sleep. She had stopped distinguishing between the two.

Yeon-shin glanced at her, then spoke.

"It's over."

"Indeed." The steward's voice was rough. He stood with his hands at his sides, dirt under his nails, dirt in the creases of his face. "It will be a sad mountain. It didn't even have a name."

"No. I mean Jeong Estate is over."

Silence. The steward's jaw worked, but no words came.

Yeon-shin turned to the Zhongnan masters. They stood apart, their robes stained with grave-earth, but their eyes were clear and hard. Martial artists to the bone. He bowed — hands clasped, waist bent. A proper salute.

"Master Precipice Sword. Master Shadowless Fist. Thank you for your help."

They returned the gesture with a sincerity that surprised him. Respect, offered to a boy of fifteen without hesitation.

"It's your disciple's household," Precipice Sword said. "We could hardly stand aside."

"Also," Shadowless Fist added, gravity heavy between his thick brows, "it is hard to say we bear no responsibility for failing to stop it."

Jung-san stood nearby, holding Hye-ah. He had not spoken since dawn. Now he looked at Yeon-shin with an expression the boy had never seen on his second brother's face.

Something almost like guilt.

"How can one apologise with a single word for things accumulated over long years?" Jung-san said. His voice was strained, thin.

*You cannot. But this is not the time.*

"When we meet again, I'll try to be a proper older brother. I'll make an effort. So you survive."

"Though I'm belatedly entering Zhongnan martial arts, I'm not exceptionally talented," Yeon-shin said. "That's how I see it."

For the first time in his life, he told the truth to an elder without softening it. The words came out clean and plain, and he felt refreshed, wondering why he had not done this before.

"Train hard," he said. "And take care of Hye-ah."

"I understand."

And so they departed. The Zhongnan masters, his second brother, and the small sleeping girl on his shoulder. Down the mountain path, into the grey morning. Gone.

Yeon-shin turned.

The steward stood with the remaining workers, bundles on their backs. Ready to move.

"Where will you go, steward?"

"I told you I had contacts. There are small branches of the Jeong bloodline throughout Namyang. The family-owned farmlands aren't only in Xinye." The steward smiled — faint, weary. "Since there are still Jeong kin alive, I should naturally serve them with the remaining assets."

*This man is too good for Jeong Estate. He always was.*

"As you know, I'm going to Desolate Sect. I hope we can see each other occasionally."

"Of course."

"I hope even the steward lives a long life. Take care of yourself."

The words left his mouth before he thought about them. Simple. Sincere. The steward's face changed — something cracking behind the careful mask of duty.

"I'll pray for your success, young master."

Farewell.

Yeon-shin walked. He did not look back.

Behind him, the steward watched the boy's retreating figure push through the tall grass and thought: *Live a long life. Have I ever heard such words from a Jeong?*

---

He had never been outside Xinye County.

The road from Namyang to Xiangyang was also the road connecting Henan and Huguang provinces — a wide dirt track beaten flat by ten thousand feet, lined with inns and teahouses and the occasional roadside shrine. Yeon-shin followed the steward's directions and kept to the main route.

He attracted attention.

"His clothes are unusual for a beggar."

"Isn't that silk? Using silk for martial clothes..."

"Silk? But that appearance..."

The porters from a passing convoy whispered as they went by. Yeon-shin walked in silence, his hand near his sword hilt. The pack on his back rode heavy against his shoulders, and he checked it every hour to make sure it was still cinched tight. That was how many people he encountered. The road was thick with merchants, travellers, convoys — and wolves.

*Troublesome.*

He could not use the mountain paths. As a novice who had just entered the martial world, getting lost in the hills would be worse than being robbed. So he walked the open road and kept his eyes sharp.

The wolves came on the second day.

Three wandering swordsmen. Rogues. The kind who haunted the highways between provinces, eyeing travellers for wealth. Two wore cracked leather jerkins sun-bleached to near white. The third had a knife in each hand and a grin that said he had used them before.

"Silk clothes on a boy. Hand over the pack and you walk away."

Yeon-shin's hand closed around the hilt. The iron blade cleared the scabbard. A diagonal cut — hip rotation, core torque, energy surging through him.

The knife-wielder's blade clattered on the road. His body followed it. The other two stumbled back, hands going to hilts — too late. The swift sword was already there.

Blood on the road-dust. Flies already gathering.

*What were they relying on to attack?*

In a world where robbery and murder were common, learning was precious. Among the martial artists he encountered on the roadside, even those with properly initiated internal energy were rare. Most fought with brute strength and the assumption that a boy travelling alone would be afraid.

He was not afraid. He had killed men in his family's courtyard. He had stood before the Blade Sect Leader. After that, highway rogues were nothing.

But each fight taught him something.

As he wielded the swift sword in real combat while using the pupil technique, his body changed. The muscles throughout his frame adapted to instantaneous explosive power. Flexibility increased. The swift sword, already fast, became faster still — until he could no longer track his own blade with his eyes. The Jeong family's pupil technique melded into his swordsmanship, and his body became something new. Not the body of a boy. The body of a weapon.

The pressure at his crown flared on the third night, while he slept under a pine tree with his pack as a pillow. He woke with his vision grey, the burning spreading down through his temples, his jaw clenched against the pain. Three heartbeats. Four. Then it passed.

*The gate is still widening.*

He sat up in the dark and pressed his thumb to the crown of his head. Warm. Always warm.

*Five years. Perhaps less.*

He pushed the thought through the door in his mind — the door behind which his father's face waited, and Nam-san's body in the corridor, and the sound of Hye-ah's weeping. The door closed. He breathed. He slept again.

One week. Hard beds, chilly air, the taste of road-grit in his teeth. A time of enduring his first outdoor sleeping, of waking before dawn to the creak of cart wheels on the road, of eating dry rice and pickled vegetables bought from roadside stalls with coin he could not afford to waste.

"Phew."

He entered an inn in Xiangyang and set down his pack. He had arrived. The city sprawled around him, loud and dense with life. Tens of thousands of people came and went daily. Thousands of types of goods were exchanged. A metropolis.

A pressure bloomed at the crown of his head — brief, sharp. His vision wavered. He blinked it away.

*Not now.*

He ordered a meal and listened.

"People gather from all over the Central Plains with dreams of success. Martial artists throwing around silver!"

"Taverns busy day and night. Everyone drowning in alcohol before the entrance exam!"

"Desolate Sect is really something."

Merchants, at the next table. Yeon-shin felt the cost of living from the lodging fee alone. His money would last another month. No more. He had thought he brought a lot, pilfered quietly from the Blade Sect's leavings.

That was when the young man sat down across from him.

Friendly smile. A sword at his waist — clearly not cheap. And on his forehead, a blue headband embroidered with a golden dragon.

A hero's headband. An accessory nobody wore anymore unless they were truly trying to show off. This one even had the dragon done in gold thread.

"Did you also come to enter Desolate Sect, young brother?"

"And you are?"

"Heon Won-chang. I came from far away Shanxi."

"Jeong Yeon-shin. From Xinye."

"Xinye? The journey must have been comfortable! I crossed swords with bandits in the green forests and raced river bandits in the water! Their swimming was fierce!"

*Comfortable is one word for it.*

"Mine was comfortable enough."

Won-chang leaned forward. He had the energy of a man who had been talking to himself for too long and finally found someone willing to listen. His mood went up and down like a kite in shifting winds — one moment boasting about his journey, the next gloomy about the difficulty of the test.

"Almost no one passes on the first try. It's called the Desolate test — like the civil service exam, but for martial artists. Whether you have a foundation or not, the moment you enter, a world of learning opens up. There must be thousands practising all kinds of martial arts just to pass."

He paused. The strength drained from his voice.

"But the walls of Desolate Sect are dizzyingly high."

Yeon-shin's food arrived — kung pao chicken, small vegetables. He had not eaten properly in days. Even the smell was welcome.

Won-chang watched him eat with a complicated expression.

"How about we take the test together tomorrow?"

"Aye. Let's do that."

*I'm glad there is a test. I will not beg help from maternal relatives I have never met.*

Resolution and youthful pride. He would not become pitiful. Even if his life depended on it.

---

The next day, they stood before Desolate Sect.

Yeon-shin was no longer the third son of Jeong Estate. A week on the road had burned that away. A swordsman who had weathered an especially harsh week for a child with wealth. The boy had become a martial artist.

Enormous.

The fortress spread its massive presence against the sky, shoulder to shoulder with the clear blue above. Double-layered walls, towering, solid as mountain stone. A moat circled it — a vast band of water. The wide-open main gate was the confidence of a place that claimed to be the finest in the martial world.

"Impregnable," Won-chang breathed.

Yeon-shin stepped forward. Before the gate, a fenced training ground. They registered at the reception desk. Yeon-shin gave his name, received a round token — thirteen — and turned to face the test.

*Noisy.*

Spectators, merchants, gamblers — the Desolate Sect let them all watch, as if inviting the world to witness and enjoy.

An examiner stood waiting inside the fence. Neat martial clothes. A condensed aura no less powerful than the masters of the Thirteen Heavens.

"The first test is the swift sword form."

Yeon-shin blinked.

"Pardon?"

---
